original_abstract:
A common mantra in amine-based post-combustion capture (PCC) is that operators must "keep the solvent clean" to minimize consumption. This strategy assumes that removing metals, degradation products, and reactive trace components captured from flue gas—such as NO2, which is often cited alongside dissolved O2 as a primary driver of degradation—will directly reduce amine losses. However, this theoretical hypothesis is largely based on laboratory experiments using fresh amines and often overlooks the true complexity of the solvent matrix. In reality, the interaction between potential metal catalysts, degradation products, and oxidizing agents requires specific chemical conditions to be met before degradation can proceed.

To test these assumptions, the degradation of the CESAR1 solvent (an aqueous blend of 3.0 M 2-amino-2-methylpropan-1-ol (AMP) and 1.5 M piperazine (PZ)) was investigated during a unique 40-month, 24/7 continuous test campaign at the Niederaussem lignite-fired power plant. Crucially, this campaign was conducted without replacing the solvent inventory. Three distinct solvent management strategies were evaluated: a) ion exchange to remove anionic and cationic compounds, b) adsorptive removal of trace elements using active carbon, and c) flue gas pretreatment with thiosulfate/sulfite to remove >80% of NO2.

The results demonstrate that "solvent cleanliness" is not a well-defined parameter and that findings from laboratory settings or short-term monoethanolamine (MEA) tests cannot be easily generalized to other solvents in industrial applications. Notably, some cleaning efforts proved counterproductive, actually increasing degradation rates or producing unnecessary waste and costs. These findings highlight the need for customized degradation management strategies that account for the specific solvent, plant design, and flue gas quality, rather than adhering to oversimplified "cleanliness" benchmarks.
humanized_abstract:
For as long as I can remember, the common wisdom when it comes to amine-based post-combustion capture (PCC) has been “keep the solvent clean”. The reasoning is that if metals, degradation products, and other reactive species (e.g., trace metals, NO2) are removed, amine consumption will decrease. While this hypothesis is largely supported by lab studies that start with “fresh” amine, the actual complexity of the solvent system is not fully considered. In particular, any number of possible interactions between potential metal catalysts, degradation products, and oxidants may require certain conditions to be met before any actual solvent degradation is observed.

In the current study, the validity of these assumptions was verified by testing the CESAR1 solvent degradation during a 40-month, 24/7 continuous test campaign at the Niederaussem lignite-fired power plant. This test was carried out without replacing the solvent inventory. The solvent management methods tested were: ion exchange to remove the anionic and cationic species, adsorptive removal of trace elements by using active carbon and flue gas pretreatment with thiosulfate/sulfite which resulted in the removal of more than 80% of NO2.

Solvent Cleanliness - Is it a Realistic Metric ? These test results demonstrate that there is no such thing as “solvent cleanliness” and that lab based short term MEA tests cannot be simply transposed to other more complex solvent systems in industrial operations. Interestingly enough, certain cleaning methods were detrimental to the desired rate of degradation, resulting in either a reduced performance, increased amount of waste and operations costs. As a consequence, the results suggest that a far more customized approach to degradation management is required, which is specific to each solvent system, the particular design of the plant and the characteristics of the flue gas. The over-simplification of “cleanliness” as a metric cannot be perpetuated based on these observations.

A common mantra in amine-based post-combustion capture (PCC) is that operators must "keep the solvent clean" to minimize consumption.
